coredata.dc:title: Systematic evaluation of graphene quantum dot toxicity to male mouse sexual behaviors, reproductive and offspring health
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Ding
Zhang, Zefei
Wu, Ying
Fu, Kai
Chen, Yang
Li, Wenhao
Chu, Maoquan
coredata.dc:description: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have attracted considerable attention across multiple fields, particularly biomedical research. However, the effects of GQDs on reproductive and offspring health in mammals are unclear. Here, we show that GQD exposure via oral gavage or intravenous injection had no effect on the frequency and timing of sexual behaviors in male mice. GQD-exposed male mice retained healthy structural and functional reproductive physiology (e.g., production and storage of healthy sperm, maintenance of normal total protein and key enzyme concentrations in testes) of the testes and epididymides, as well as normal testosterone levels. Female mice housed with GQD-exposed males produced first, second, and subsequent litters of healthy pups without obvious differences to females housed with buffer-treated males. These findings may be explained by the low toxicity of GQDs in germ cells and their rapid excretion after exposure in mice, mainly via the urine and/or feces; GQDs, even at high doses, are virtually undetectable in male mouse testis, epididymis, and brain. Our findings reveal the short- and long-term effects of GQD exposure on male mouse sexual behaviors, reproductive activity, and offspring development and indicate the potential mechanisms of action of GQDs to provide further insight into their bio-safety.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Intravenous injection
Oral gavage
Male sexual behaviors
Male reproductive ability
Offspring health

xocs:hub-sec.xocs:hub-sec-title: Biomaterials at the Nanoscale for Diagnostic Systems

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Introduction
Materials and methods
Cell line and animals
Detection of GQD toxicity in male mouse germ cells
Bio-distribution of GQDs in mice after intravenous injection or oral gavage
GQD exposure methods
Evaluation of reproductive capacity in GQD-Exposed male mice
Analysis of the health of offspring produced by GQD-Exposed mice
Evaluation of fetal deformities among offspring
Blood analysis of GQD-Exposed male mice
Histological analysis of testes and epididymes of GQD-Exposed male mice
Detection of testosterone level in serum from GQD-Exposed male mice
Detection of sperm quality in epididymes of GQD-Exposed male mice
Analysis of total protein and major enzymatic activity in testes and epididymes
Statistical analysis
Results and discussion
Effect of GQD exposure on male mouse sexual behaviors
Short-term effects of GQD exposure on male mouse reproductive and offspring health
Long-term effects of GQD exposure on male mouse reproductive and offspring health
Sex ratio of female to male offspring produced by GQD-Exposed male mice
Malformations in fetuses produced by GQD-Exposed male mice
The main reasons for the lack of effect of GQD exposure on male mouse reproduction and offspring health
Conclusion
Acknowledgements
Supplementary data
References

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Reproductive ability of and first litters of pups produced by male mice orally gavaged or intravenously injected with different doses of GQDs or PBS and housed with unexposed females 1 day after the last dose. (a) Reproductive ability of male mice and body weight and survival rates of pups (values expressed as mean ± SD). (b) Representative mouse parents and their first litters of pups at 0–24 h after birth. (c) Change in pup body weights over time (growth of pups) in the 30 days after birth. (d) Organ coefficients and (e) serum biochemistry and hematology data for pups at 30 days after birth. Error bars indicate the standard error of the mean based on all pups within a litter (Fig. 1c and d) or on four pups per group (Fig. 1e).

Reproductive ability of and first litters of pups produced by male mice orally gavaged or intravenously injected with different doses of GQDs or PBS and housed with unexposed females 30 days after the last dose. (a) Reproductive ability of male mice and body weight and survival rates of pups (values expressed as mean ± SD). (b) Representative mouse parents and their first litters of pups at 0–24 h after birth. (c) Change in pup body weights over time (growth of pups) in the 30 days after birth. (d) Organ coefficients and (e) serum biochemistry and hematology data for pups at 30 days after birth. Error bars indicate the standard error of the mean based on all pups in a litter (Fig. 2c) or on four pups per group (Fig. 2d).

Reproductive ability of and second and third litters of pups produced by the male mice orally gavaged or intravenously injected with different doses of GQDs or PBS and housed with unexposed females 30 days after the last dose. (a) Reproductive ability of male mice and body weight and survival rates of pups (values expressed as mean ± SD). (b) Change in pup body weight over time (pup growth) in the 30 days after birth. (c) Organ coefficients of offspring. (d) H&E-stained slices of main organs from offspring. Error bars indicate the standard error of the mean based on all pups in a litter (Fig. 3b) or on four pups per group (Fig. 3c).

Serum biochemistry and hematology data for the first, second, and third litters of offspring at 30 days after birth. Offspring were produced by male mice orally gavaged or intravenously injected with different doses of GQDs or PBS. The significance levels (*P < 0.05 and **P < 0.01) indicate statistically significant differences between the two groups shown in the corresponding histograms. Error bars indicate the standard error of the mean based on four mouse pups per group.

Malformed mouse fetuses and corresponding placentas from female mice mated with GQD-exposed and control male mice. (a) True-color images of fetuses and placentas. All photographs are at the same magnification. (b) Numbers of total fetuses and deformed fetuses in each group (eight pairs of parents per group). (c) Ratios of normal fetuses and placentas to total fetuses in each group. Bar: 0.5 cm. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Toxicity of GQDs in mouse male germ cells. Viability of (a) GC-1 cells and (b) TM3 cells following incubation with various concentrations (0.05–0.5 mg/mL) of GQDs for 2–48 h. Error bars indicate the standard error of the mean based on five experiments per group.

Biodistribution of GQDs in male mice 6, 12, and 24 h post-exposure and suggested mechanisms for the lack of effect of GQD exposure on male mouse reproduction. (a) Schematic illustration of GQD-Cu2+ complex formation. (b) Stability of GQDs-Cu2+ in SGF or SB. Time-dependent biodistribution of GQDs in male mice after (c) intravenous injection and (d) oral gavage. Error bars indicate the standard error of the mean based three mice per experiment. (e–f) Schematic illustrations of the suggested mechanisms by which GQDs-treated male mice retained normal sexual behavior and reproductivity: (e) GQDs exhibit low toxicity in germ cells; (f) GQDs cannot easily penetrate the blood–brain and blood–testis barriers, do not damage the main organs, and are rapidly excreted from the body.

Characteristics of testes and epididymes of male mice 30 days after oral gavage or intravenous injection with GQDs or PBS. (a) Morphologies and (b) organ coefficients of the testes and epididymes of male mice exposed to different doses of GQDs and controls. Error bars indicate the standard error of the mean based on three mice per group. (c) H&E-stained slices of testes and epididymes of male mice in high-dose GQD and PBS groups at 30 days after exposure. (d) Analysis of total protein and key enzyme concentrations in testes of mice orally gavaged or intravenously injected with various doses of GQDs and controls. Values are expressed as means ± SD. n = 5 per group.

Sperm quality and testosterone level of male mice 30 days after GQD exposure and controls. Male mice were orally gavaged or intravenously injected with various doses of GQDs or PBS. (a) Sperm morphologies. (b) Various parameters of sperms [1]. sperm motility (unit: %) [2], sperm progression (unit: %) [3], VAP (unit: μm/s) [4], VSL (unit: μm/s) [5], VCL (unit: μm/s) [6], ALH (unit: μm) [7], BCF (unit: Hz) [8], STR (unit: %) [9], LIN (unit: %) [10–13], and sperm activity [10] (rapid [11], medium [12], slow, and [13] static) (unit: %). (c) Serum testosterone levels. Error bars indicate the standard error of the mean based on three mice per group.

General health of male mice following oral gavage or intravenous injection with various doses of GQDs and controls. (a) Male mouse body weight over time during the 30 days after exposure. (b) Morphologies of the main organs, including heart, liver, spleen, lung, and kidney. (c) H&E-stained liver and kidney slices from male mice in the high-dose GQD and PBS groups at 30 days after exposure. (d) Main organ coefficients of male mice at 30 days after exposure. (e) Serum biochemistry and hematology data from male mice at 30 days after exposure. The significance level, *P < 0.05, represents the statistical difference between two groups shown in the corresponding histograms. Error bars indicate the standard error of the mean based on three mice per group.

Schematic illustration of animal studies of the reproductive ability of male mice and mouse wellbeing after GQD exposure. (a) Evaluation of GQD-exposed male mouse sexual behaviors, reproductive and offspring wellbeing, offspring malformation rate, and sex ratio. (b) Studies of the toxicity of GQDs in male germ cells, in vivo biodistribution and excretory routes of GQDs, testes and epididymes structure and function, sperm quality and testosterone levels, and liver and kidney function (blood analysis) of GQD-exposed male mice. Male mice were orally gavaged with low (60 mg/kg/day), intermediate (100 mg/kg/day), or high (300 mg/kg/day) doses of GQDs or injected via the tail vein with low (25 mg/kg), intermediate (75 mg/kg), or high (150 mg/kg) doses of GQDs for 10 consecutive days. Age-matched female mice were not mated with males prior to the experiments, and were not treated with GQDs.
Frequency and timing of sexual behaviors in GQD-exposed male mice towards non-exposed female mice at 1 and 30 days after exposure.

row.entry: Male mice chasing female mice during 24 h
Male mice climbing onto the back of females during 24 h
Average chasing time in each time (s)

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Values are expressed as mean ± SD (n = 4 per group).

head.ce:title: Systematic evaluation of graphene quantum dot toxicity to male mouse sexual behaviors, reproductive and offspring health

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Biomedical Multidisciplinary Innovation Research Institute and Research Center for Translational Medicine at Shanghai East Hospital, School of Life Sciences and Technology, Tongji University, Shanghai 200092, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Biomedical Multidisciplinary Innovation Research Institute and Research Center for Translational Medicine at Shanghai East Hospital
School of Life Sciences and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Biophysics, Chinese Academy of Science, Beijing 100101, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: University of Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing 100049, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: University of Chinese Academy of Sciences

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have attracted considerable attention across multiple fields, particularly biomedical research. However, the effects of GQDs on reproductive and offspring health in mammals are unclear. Here, we show that GQD exposure via oral gavage or intravenous injection had no effect on the frequency and timing of sexual behaviors in male mice. GQD-exposed male mice retained healthy structural and functional reproductive physiology (e.g., production and storage of healthy sperm, maintenance of normal total protein and key enzyme concentrations in testes) of the testes and epididymides, as well as normal testosterone levels. Female mice housed with GQD-exposed males produced first, second, and subsequent litters of healthy pups without obvious differences to females housed with buffer-treated males. These findings may be explained by the low toxicity of GQDs in germ cells and their rapid excretion after exposure in mice, mainly via the urine and/or feces; GQDs, even at high doses, are virtually undetectable in male mouse testis, epididymis, and brain. Our findings reveal the short- and long-term effects of GQD exposure on male mouse sexual behaviors, reproductive activity, and offspring development and indicate the potential mechanisms of action of GQDs to provide further insight into their bio-safety.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Introduction
ce:section.ce:para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), also referred to as zero-dimensional graphene nanosheets have attracted considerable attention in the fields of materials science, chemistry, and physics [2], as well as in biomedical research [3]. In biomedical fields, GQDs have been used for in vivo drug delivery [4], as biosensors in biomedical detection assays [5], and as photosensitizers and photothermal agents for in vivo cancer photodynamic and photothermal therapies [6–9]. Given their significant potential for in vivo applications [3–9], the toxicology of GQDs requires comprehensive evaluation before any clinical use. Most current studies have shown that GQDs do not exhibit significant cytotoxicity in cell cultures [10–15]. Although these findings do not indicate adverse effects of GQDs on cellular physiological activities, the possibility of DNA damage in cells following the release of reactive oxygen species (ROS) is of considerable concern [16]. GQDs have also been shown to induce inflammatory responses, apoptosis, and autophagy in human macrophages via p38 mitogen-activated protein kinase and nuclear factor-kB signaling pathways [17]. Although most reports have shown that GQDs do not cause appreciable toxicity in animal studies [11,18,19], one study has reported that GQDs disrupted embryonic development in zebrafish at concentrations exceeding 50 μg/mL [20].

Therefore, the toxicity of GQDs requires further extensive investigation because the clinical application of GQDs cannot progress until critical issues regarding their biosafety have been sufficiently addressed. GQDs are nanoparticles with a thickness of only a single atom and are ∼1–20 nm in lateral dimension size, and therefore may enter the main organs and reduce the viability of germ cells after exposure [11,18,21]. For example, GQDs have been shown to block autophagic flux by decreasing the amount and enzymatic activity of cathepsin B and inhibiting lysosome proteolytic capacity in GC-2 and TM4 cells [21]. The reduced viability of germ cells might presents a potential hazard to male reproduction [21]. Given the importance of retaining normal fertility, the evaluation of reproductive and developmental toxicity is essential during risk assessment of chemicals [22]. Although adverse effects of manufactured nanomaterials on reproductive activity and offspring health have been noted in recent years [23–36], research in this field remains in its infancy. The reproductive output of animals has been shown to decrease after exposure to nano-titanium dioxide (TiO2) [25–29], copper [30,31] and silver nanoparticles [31], gold nanoparticles [32], Fe3O4 nanoparticles [33], titanium silicon oxide (TiSiO4) nanoparticles [34], C60 [35], and carbon nanotubes [36]. For instance, the ingestion of copper or silver nanoparticles (20 nm) has been shown to slow the development of Drosophila melanogaster, and the ingestion of copper also decreased sperm competition in Drosophila [31]. Oral expose to nano-sized TiO2 (<25 nm) may also decrease serum testosterone levels and sperm count in the epididymis of adult rats [28]. It should be noted, however, that the majority of these studies were performed in Zebrafish [25,26], Daphnia magna [29], Drosophila melanogaster [31], Caenorhabditis elegans [32], or other small animals [27,30,33–35], and not in mammals.

Given that reproductive and developmental toxicity are important factors in overall toxicology [37], it is particularly important to assess the reproductive risk and offspring health after exposure to GQDs. To the best of our knowledge, it has not previously been reported whether GQDs exert sexual behavior or reproductive toxicities in mammals or their offspring.

In this study, the ICR mouse strain was used as a model to determine whether GQDs exhibit toxicity to mammalian sexual behaviors and reproduction because of its reproductive vigor, rapid growth, and athletic physique [38]. Male ICR mice were utilized as they lack the periodic fluctuation of physiological indexes observed in females. In addition, hormone levels and enzymatic activities in male mice are stable compared with those of female mice [39]. The novel points of this study are as follows: we investigated the cytotoxicity of GQDs in the TM3 and GC-1 male germ cell lines, and short- and long-term effects of GQD exposure on male mouse sexual behaviors and reproductive ability following orally gavage or intravenous injection of high-dose GQDs. We also investigated the in vivo biodistribution of Cu2+-labeled GQDs in male mice, as well as the health of the first, second, and subsequent litters of offspring produced from GQDs-treated mice. Interesting results are presented, such as the finding that GQD exposure had little effect on the structure and reproductive functions of the testes and epididymides, or on the frequency and timing of sexual behaviors of male mice. Oral and intravenous administration routes were selected to investigate the in vivo toxicity of GQDs as graphene nanosheets have previously been used for intravenous delivery of water-soluble anticancer drugs (e.g., doxorubicin) as well as oral delivery of water-insoluble anticancer drugs (e.g., flavonoids) [40,41]. In addition, these two administration methods are the most commonly used routes in clinical practice. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first report of the influence of GQD exposure on male mouse reproductive and offspring health. The results presented in this study may significantly contribute to the medical application of GQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Materials and methods
Cell line and animals
ce:section.ce:para: GC-1 spg(ts) (ATCC # CRL-2053) and TM3 (ATCC # CRL-1714) cell lines were obtained from the American Type Culture Collection (ATCC). Female and male ICR mice (6–7 weeks old) were purchased from the Shanghai Sipper-BK Lab Animal Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China) and were housed in a specific pathogen-free environment on a 12-h light (lights on at 7:00 a.m.): 12-h dark cycle and provided with food and water ad libitum. All animal experiments were performed in accordance with the University of Tongji Institutional Animal Care and Use Committee guidelines.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of GQD toxicity in male mouse germ cells
ce:section.ce:para: GC-1 cells were seeded into 96-well plates (∼7 × 103 cells/well) and cultured in a CO2 incubator at 37 °C using Dulbecco's modified Eagle's medium (DMEM) containing penicillin (100 U/mL), streptomycin (100 U/mL), and fetal bovine serum (FBS) (10%). After 24 h of culture, the medium was removed and cells were cultured with GQDs (0.05, 0.1, 0.3, and 0.5 mg/mL) in serum-free medium (200 μL per well) at 37 °C for 2, 12, 24, or 48 h. Cells without GQDs were used as controls. After incubation at each time point, the medium was removed and 100 μL CellTiter-Glo® luminescent cell viability assay reagent was added to each well, and the cells were incubated for 15 min at ambient temperature. The luminescent intensities of cells were detected, and cell viability was calculated as I Cells to be detected × 100/I Untreated cells, where I was the luminescent intensity. Each experiment was repeated five times.

The viabilities of TM3 cells after exposure to GQDs were also measured. TM3 cells were cultured in Nutrient Mixture F-12 (DMEM/F12) containing penicillin (100 U/mL), streptomycin (100 U/mL), and FBS (10%). The GQD concentrations and cell viability analysis method at each time point of incubation were as described above.
The toxicity of GQDs prepared by cutting down graphene oxide in mouse germ cells was also measured as described above.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Bio-distribution of GQDs in mice after intravenous injection or oral gavage

ce:list-item.ce:para: Preparation of Cu2+ ion-labeled GQDs. GQD aqueous solution (10 mg/mL, 8 mL) was mixed with CuSO4 aqueous solution (100 mM, 2 mL) at ambient temperature for 30 min and then centrifuged at 10,000 rcf for 30 min. The precipitate (GQDs-Cu2+) was washed three times with water and subsequently dispersed in phosphate buffered saline (PBS). The amount of Cu2+ ions absorbed on GQDs was measured using an inductively coupled plasma optical emission spectrometer (ICP-OES) (iCAP6300, Thermo Fisher, USA). For calculating the concentration of Cu2+ in samples, a series of standard CuCl2 aqueous solutions was prepared at concentrations ranging from 0 to 10 μg/mL. The standard curve was established according to the relationship between the emission intensity at the wavelength of 244.7 nm detected by ICP-OES and the corresponding concentrations of Cu atoms. The standard curve is as follows: C = 268.46 × I+6.76 (R2 = 0.999). Where C is the concentration of Cu atoms, and I is the emission intensity of Cu atoms. The concentrations of Cu2+ in samples were detected by ICP-OES and then calculated according to the standard curve. The mass of Cu2+ ions chelated by GQDs was calculated as the total mass of Cu2+ ions in the CuSO4 aqueous solution (7.00 mg/mL, 2 mL) minus the mass of Cu2+ ions in the supernatant (0.63 mg/mL, 10 mL). Each experiment was repeated in triplicate.

Stability of Cu2+ ions absorbed on GQDs. A PBS-dispersed GQDs-Cu2+ suspension (10 mg/mL, 400 μL) was mixed with simulated blood (SB) (600 μL) or simulated gastric fluid (SGF) (600 μL) in a dialysis bag (MWCO 1000 Da) and dialyzed against SB (30 mL) or SGF (30 mL). At regular intervals (1, 6, or 24 h), 1 mL of dialysates was withdrawn from the receptor compartments for each time point and replaced with 1 mL of SB or SGF. The concentrations of Cu2+ ions in the receptor solutions were measured by ICP-OES.

In vivo bio-distribution of GQDs. For intravenous injection, ICR mice (n = 12) were intravenously injected with PBS-dispersed GQDs-Cu2+ (75 mg/kg, 200 μL) and sacrificed at 1 h, 6 h, 24 h or 7 days after injection (n = 3 per time point). The mouse blood, urine and feces were collected, and heart, liver, spleen, lung, kidney, testes, epididymes, brain and posterior leg bone were resected and weighted, and then ground into a mash (except urine), respectively. The urine and the mash of each sample were subsequently immersed in aqua regia (5 mL per sample) for 48 h. The suspensions were concentrated to 1 mL for each sample. The samples were next diluted with distilled water to 5 mL for Cu2+ analysis by ICP-OES. For oral gavage, mice were received PBS-dispersed GQDs-Cu2+ (150 mg/kg, 500 μL) and were sacrificed at 1 h, 6 h, 24 h, or 7 days after gavage (n = 3 per time point). The subsequent procedures for organ resection and Cu2+ detection were as described above. As controls, three mice without injection and gavage were sacrificed, and the concentrations of Cu2+ ions in the urine, feces, and main organs and tissues were analyzed using the same method.

ce:section.ce:para: The in vivo biodistribution after intravenous injection of GQDs prepared by the top-down method was also investigated as described above. A total of 39 mice were used in these experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: GQD exposure methods
ce:section.ce:para: To study mouse reproductive and offspring health, 152 male mice were exposed to PBS-dispersed GQDs or PBS alone, or were unexposed, as detailed in the following exposure methods. (1) Oral gavage: 72 male mice were orally gavaged with PBS-dispersed GQDs every 24 h for 10 days. The doses at each time point were 0 (PBS), 60, 100, and 300 mg GQDs/kg mouse (500 μL per mouse). In the following sections, these groups are termed control (PBS gavage), low-, intermediate-, and high-dose groups, respectively. Among these four groups, mouse numbers were 14, 18, 18, and 22, respectively. (2) Intravenous injection: 72 male mice were intravenously injected with GQDs at doses of 0 (PBS), 25, 75, and 150 mg GQDs/kg mouse (200 μL per mouse). In the following sections, these groups are termed control (PBS injection), low-, medium-, and high-dose groups for intravenous injection, respectively. Mouse numbers for each dosing group were the same as for the oral gavage groups. In addition, eight male mice were not exposed to GQDs or PBS. These 152 treated or untreated mice were used in the following experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of reproductive capacity in GQD-Exposed male mice

ce:list-item.ce:para: Housing of male mice with female mice. Of the 72 male mice in the oral gavage groups, 48 were housed with non-exposed female mice at a sex ratio of 1:1 at day 1 (n = 8) or day 30 (n = 40) after the last gavage. One squirrel cage was used to house one male and one female mouse. Of the 72 male mice in the intravenous injection groups, 48 were also housed with non-exposed females as described above. For the non-exposed group, eight male mice were housed with non-exposed females 32 days after purchase. Male and female mice used for these experiments had never been previously mated. (2) Monitoring of sexual behaviors among GQD-exposed male mice. A video camera (Yi Home Camera, Yi Technology Co. Ltd., Shanghai, China) was installed 2 m above the squirrel cages and recording started as soon as the males were housed with the females. Male mouse behavior towards female mice and any sexual intercourse were recorded over a 24-h period. (3) Recording of pregnancies and birth by female mice and pup parameters in the first, second, and third litters. The pregnancies of female mice were recorded by observation. The time interval between housing with males and delivery of litters, pup numbers at 0–24 h and 30 days after birth (survival rate = [pup number at 30 days after birth] × 100%/[pup number at 0–24 h after birth]), pup weights at 0–24 h after birth, and sex ratio of female to male pups at 30 days after birth were recorded. Males and females were continuously housed together after the first litters of pups were born. Females achieving second and third pregnancies and birth, as well as the pup parameters described above, were recorded.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of the health of offspring produced by GQD-Exposed mice
ce:section.ce:para: These offspring were the same as those described in the previous section. (1) Recording of pup weight and morphology during growth. Weights and images of offspring were recorded at 1, 7, 14, 21, and 30 day(s) after birth. Offspring images during growth were captured using an iPhone 6 (Apple, USA). (2) Detection of kidney and liver function and hematology parameters in offspring. Thirty days after birth, one pup was randomly selected from each litter and anaesthetized by ether (4 pups from each group of four litters). Blood (∼0.8 mL) was collected from the retro-orbital sinus and divided into two aliquots. One aliquot was centrifuged and serum was collected for the detection of kidney and liver functions by analyzing the levels of nine chemistry parameters (albumin [ALB], globulin [GLOB], albumin/globulin ratio [A/G], aspartate aminotransferase [AST], alanine aminotransferase [ALT], alkaline phosphatase [ALP], total protein [TP], creatinine [CRE] and urea). The other aliquot was used to evaluate the following hematology parameters: white blood cells (WBC), red blood cells (RBC), hematocrit (HCT), mean corpuscular volume (MCV), mean corpuscular hemoglobin (MCH), mean corpuscular hemoglobin concentration (MCHC), hemoglobin (HGB) and platelets (PLT). Blood parameters were measured at KingMed Diagnostics (Shanghai, China). (3) Histological examination of offspring organs. Following blood collection, pups were sacrificed and the main organs (liver, spleen, lung, and kidney) were resected and weighed. Organs were fixed in 4% paraformaldehyde at 4 °C for 24 h, embedded in paraffin, and sliced into 5 μm sections for Hematoxylin-eosin (H&E) staining. Sections were imaged using an upright digital microscope (DM 4000: Leica, Nussloch, Germany).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of fetal deformities among offspring
ce:section.ce:para: Approximately 2 days before giving birth, pregnant female mice in the oral gavage (n = 24), intravenous injection (n = 24), and non-exposed (n = 8) groups were anaesthetized using 4% chloral hydrate by intraperitoneal injection. Mouse abdomens were carefully incised and fetuses were removed for observation. Deformed fetuses were recorded and images captured using an iPhone 6.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Blood analysis of GQD-Exposed male mice
ce:section.ce:para: Twenty-four male mice in the oral gavage (n = 12) and intravenous injection (n = 12) groups were anaesthetized by ether at 30 days after the last gavage or injection. Mice were weighed and blood was collected for the evaluation of kidney and liver function and hematology parameters using the methods described previously.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Histological analysis of testes and epididymes of GQD-Exposed male mice
ce:section.ce:para: Following blood collection, mice were sacrificed and the testes and epididymes were resected and weighed. Tissue morphologies were imaged using an iPhone 6, and tissues were fixed, embedded, and sliced into ultra-thin sections for H&E staining and optical imaging as described previously. Other organs (liver, spleen, lung, and kidney) were also resected for weighing and H&E staining and imaging.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of testosterone level in serum from GQD-Exposed male mice
ce:section.ce:para: Twenty-four male mice treated with GQDs or PBS were anaesthetized at 30 days after the last oral gavage (n = 12) or intravenous injection (n = 12). Blood (∼0.8 per mouse) was collected from the retro-orbital sinus, and serum was separated by centrifugation. Testosterone levels in serum were detected according to the manufacturer's protocol for the testosterone TELISA kit.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of sperm quality in epididymes of GQD-Exposed male mice
ce:section.ce:para: Following blood collection, mice were sacrificed and the epididymes and testes were resected. The epididymes were immersed in 3 mL Roswell Park Memorial Institute (RPMI-1640) medium containing 10% fetal calf serum (FCS) and cut into pieces. The pieces were gently shaken in medium at 37 °C until turbid suspensions were obtained. The suspensions were diluted with fresh medium for sperm quality analysis. First, sperm morphologies were imaged using an upright microscope (Eclipse TE2000-S, Nikon, Japan), and videos of sperm were captured using an iPad mini (Apple). Second, parameters of sperm quality were measured using an IVOS Sperm Analyzer (Hamilton Thorne Biosciences). These parameters included motility, progression, average path velocity (VAP), straight-line velocity (VSL), eurvilinear velocity (VCL), amplitude of lateral head displacement (ALH), beat-cross frequency (BCF), straightness (STR), and linearity (LIN), and sperm activity, classified as grade a (rapid), grade b (medium), grade c (slow), and grade d (static).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of total protein and major enzymatic activity in testes and epididymes
ce:section.ce:para: Mouse testes and epididymes isolated as described previously were immersed in PBS and homogenized to produce 10% w/v and 4% w/v suspensions, respectively. The concentration of glutathione peroxidase (GSH-Px), lactate dehydrogenase (LDH), acid phosphatase (ACP), and the total protein (TP) in testes, and the activity of a-glucosidase in epididymes, were determined according to the manufacturer's protocols of the appropriate assay kits.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Statistical analysis
ce:section.ce:para: Statistical significance was determined by analysis of variance (ANOVA) followed by Tukey's post hoc test, and a P-value < 0.05 was considered statistically significant while P < 0.01 was considered highly significant.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Results and discussion
Effect of GQD exposure on male mouse sexual behaviors
ce:section.ce:para: The GQDs used in this study were prepared by pyrolysis of l-glutamic acid powder at 250 °C (bottom-up method) and by cutting down graphene oxide (top-down method). In addition to the special note, GQDs were prepared by pyrolysis of l-glutamic acid. These GQDs were negatively charged (−10.6 ± 0.9 eV) ultra-small nanoparticles (5.25 ± 1.63 nm) with a graphene sheet-like structure containing carboxyl and hydroxyl groups, and emitting blue fluorescence (detailed characterizations are described in Figs. S1 and S2 and the supplementary results). Two routes of GQD exposure were used in this study: oral gavage and intravenous injection. For gavage, male mice were orally administered phosphate buffered saline (PBS)-dispersed GQDs for 10 consecutive days at low (60 mg/kg body per mouse per day, total 600 mg/kg), intermediate (100 mg/kg body per mouse per day, total 1 g/kg), or high (300 mg/kg body per mouse per day, total 3 g/kg) doses. For intravenous injection, male mice were injected via the tail vein with PBS-dispersed GQDs at low (25 mg/kg), intermediate (75 mg/kg), or high (150 mg/kg) doses. The maximum doses administered by these routes were significantly higher than those reported previously [18]. To investigate the effects of GQDs on male mouse sexual behaviors and reproduction, exposed male mice were housed with non-exposed female mice. Schematic illustrations of the experimental designs are shown in Scheme 1 and Fig. S3.

In both oral gavage and intravenous injection groups, GQD-exposed male mice that received low, intermediate, and high doses of GQDs exhibited normal sexual behavior after exposure. For example, all male mice in the high-dose gavage group at 1 day after the last exposure exhibited energetic willingness to mate with non-exposed female mice, with 40.00 ± 10.49 incidents of chasing behavior and 19.75 ± 15.84 incidents of mounting over a 24-h period. The total chasing and mounting time was 93.75 ± 34.02 s and 47.25 ± 40.52 s, respectively. The average frequencies of these sexual behaviors in GQD-exposed males were similar to those of control animals (mice observed 1 day after PBS gavage) (Table 1 ). When GQD-exposed male mice were initially housed with non-exposed females 30 days after the last gavage, total observed behavioral times were 32.25 ± 16.94 s for chasing and 21.75 ± 6.24 s for mounting, similar to those of control animals (mice observed 30 days after PBS gavage) (Table 1).

For the intravenous injection groups at 1 or 30 days after high-dose injection of GQDs, no statistically significant differences were observed in the average frequencies of sexual behaviors compared with the control group (mice intravenously injected with PBS) (Table 1). In addition, male mice in the intravenous injection groups exhibited similar sexual behaviors to those in the oral gavage groups.

These results indicate that GQD exposure via oral gavage or intravenous injection, even at high doses, had no influence on the sexual behaviors of male mice. Mouse sexual psychology and sexual function may therefore be considered normal following exposure to high-dose GQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Short-term effects of GQD exposure on male mouse reproductive and offspring health
ce:section.ce:para: We investigated the reproductive ability of GQD-exposed male mice housed with non-exposed female mice. Of note, male mice in both the oral gavage and intravenously injection groups were capable of impregnating females when housed together 1 day after the last gavage or intravenous injection. Females housed with males exposed to even high doses of GQDs all successfully produced litters at 25.00 ± 5.66 days (oral gavage) or 23.00 ± 6.93 days (intravenous injection) after housing, similar to those in the control groups (Fig. 1 a and b). Furthermore, parameters including the number of pups in each litter were similar between GQD and control groups (Fig. 1a). For example, females housed with GQD-exposed males produced 12.00 ± 2.94 pups (oral gavage) or 10.50 ± 3.00 pups (intravenous injection) per litter, and the average body weight of pups 0–24 h after birth ranged from 1.69 ± 0.13 g to 1.74 ± 0.08 g, respectively. Pups produced by GQD-exposure groups were considered healthy as their survival ratios and growth rates in the 30 days after birth, as well as organ coefficients at 30 days after birth, were similar to those in the control groups (Fig. 1a, c and d). In addition, no significant difference was observed in the growth rates of pups between the oral gavage and intravenous injection groups.

Blood analysis of pups at 30 days after birth further demonstrated that pups produced by GQD-exposed male mice were considered healthy, as liver and kidney function and routine blood indexes were normal. As shown in Fig. 1e, ALB, GLOB, A/G, AST, ALT, ALP, TP, CRE, and urea levels were not statistically different between the GQD-exposed and control groups. Furthermore, hematology counts (WBC, RBC, HCT, MCV, MCH, MCHC, HGB, and PLT) were not significantly different between GQD-exposed mice and control mice.

These findings indicate that male mice retained strong reproductive ability at 1 day after the last oral gavage or intravenous injection, implying that the activity of mature sperm in male mice appeared to be unaffected by high-dose GQD exposure, as exposed mice were capable of producing healthy pups.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Long-term effects of GQD exposure on male mouse reproductive and offspring health
ce:section.ce:para: The sperm formation and maturation period for mice is approximately 34 days [42,43]. During this period, sperm undergo subtle morphological, biochemical, and/or physiological changes in the testes and epididymis [42]. If spermatogenesis in the testes and sperm maturation in the epididymes are disrupted by exposure to external agents, such as GQDs, male mice may exhibit abnormal reproductive ability. Therefore, it is important to investigate the long-term effect of GQD exposure on male mouse reproductive ability.

For the results described here and the following sections, GQD- or PBS-exposed male mice that had received the last gavage dose or intravenous injection 30 days previously were housed with female mice for periods of more than 3 months.

The results showed that all females housed with GQD-exposed males from 30 days post-exposure conceived and gave birth to pups. No significant differences were observed in the time interval from when animals were housed together to parturition, in the pup number in each litter, and the average body weight of pups between the GQD-exposed and control groups (Figs. 2a and 3 a).

Following production of the first litter of offspring, non-exposed female mice were housed continuously with the same GQD-exposed male mice. All male mice in both the oral gavage and intravenous injection groups continued to exhibit mounting behavior. Even 4 months after the final oral gavage or injection dose, GQD-exposed male mice exhibited similar sexual behavior to age-matched PBS-treated male mice.

Importantly, all mouse dams described in these experiments exhibited normal parturition and successful delivery of second, third, and subsequent litters of pups. Even when continuously housed with male mice that received high doses of GQDs by either administration route, female mice retained normal reproductive capacity and pup numbers and average pup body weight in the second and third litters of offspring were not statistically different from the offspring produced by control mice (Figs. 2a and 3a).

It should be noted that the average interval between the final GQD dose and the birth of the second and third litters of pups was >1.5 months, with a maximum interval of about 3 months. Therefore, the results presented here indicate that the formation and maturation of sperm by GQD-exposed male mice appeared to be unaffected by high-dose GQD exposure, as all male mice orally gavaged or intravenously injected with GQDs maintained normal reproductive ability.

The effects of GQD exposure on the health of mouse offspring were as follows.
(1)
Growth rate of the first litter of pups. Female mice housed with GQD-exposed male mice produced at least three litters of offspring. The growth of pups produced by female mice mated with GQD-exposed or PBS-treated male mice was evaluated and, as shown in Fig. 2b and Fig S4, the first litters of pups were found to be normal, showing a pink or red color 0 – 24 h after birth. All pups appeared energetic and grew normally. Even in pups produced by males gavaged with high doses of GQDs, the appearance and common behaviors (e.g., sucking and walking) of pups in the first litters were similar to those in the control group. Furthermore, no statistically significant difference was observed in the growth rates of pups between GQD and PBS gavage groups (Fig. 2c).

The first litters of pups produced by female mice mated with males intravenously injected with low, medium, or high doses of GQDs also exhibited a similar appearance and growth rate to pups produced in the control group (Fig. 2b, c, and Fig S4), and no difference was shown between the groups of male mice housed with females 1 and 30 days after the last dose.

These results indicate that the first litter of mouse offspring displayed normal growth, even in litters produced by male mice exposed to high doses of GQD 30 days previously.
(2)
Histology of the main organs of the first litter of offspring. Normal organ development is an important indicator of the health of animals. As shown in Fig. 2d, organ coefficients (ratio of organ weight to body weight) of the first litters of pups at 30 days after birth were not influenced by GQD exposure. H&E staining showed that the histological structures of the main organs from the first litter of mouse offspring in GQD-exposure groups were similar to those of the offspring in control groups (Fig. 2e). For example, normal hepatocytes, sinusoids, and central veins were observed in the livers of litters produced both by males exposed to high doses of GQDs and males treated with PBS.
(3)
Effect of GQD exposure on the health of second and third litters of mouse offspring. As described previously, all female mice in this study were capable of producing second and third litters of offspring following housing with the same GQD-exposed male mice (Fig. 3a). The health of second and subsequent litters of mouse pups could potentially be influenced by GQD exposure, if GQDs were shown to exert potentially toxic effects in the reproductive system of exposed mouse sires. Therefore, we evaluated the health of the second and third litters of mouse offspring using the same methods as described for the first litters of offspring.

Growth rates among pups from the second and third litters of offspring in GQD-exposed groups were similar to those of pups in control groups (Fig. 3b, Figs. S5 and S6). For male mice gavaged or injected with high doses of GQDs, the average body weights of the third litters of offspring at 30 days after birth were 20.60 ± 1.37 g and 19.00 ± 3.71 g, respectively. At this time point, the average body weights of the third litters of offspring produced by control groups were 19.20 ± 4.03 g and 20.95 ± 4.41 g, respectively. No statistically significant differences were observed in the growth rates of pups between the first, second, and third litters in both GQD-exposed and control groups (Figs. 2c and 3b).

In addition, no statistically significant differences were observed in the main organ coefficients of the second and third litters of offspring at 30 days after birth between GQD-exposed groups and control groups (Fig. 3c). Organs such as the heart, liver, spleen, lung, and kidney showed no obvious pathological changes in litters from GQD-exposed groups compared with those of control litters (Fig. 3d). These results further indicate the health of different litters of mouse offspring following exposure of the fathers to high-dose GQDs via oral gavage or intravenous injection.
(4)
Liver and kidney function and hematological parameters of the first, second, and third litters of offspring. We evaluated the liver and kidney function of the first litter of pups at 30 days after birth. As shown in Fig. 4, a statistically significant difference in ALP was observed between GQD (high dose) and PBS (control) oral gavage groups (P < 0.05). No statistically significant difference between GQD-exposed and PBS groups was observed for other parameters (ALB, GLOB, A/G, AST, ALT, TP, CRE, and urea) (P > 0.05). For GLOB levels, a statistically significant difference was observed between low- and high-dose GQD groups (P < 0.05). Of note, liver and kidney parameter levels were all within the normal range [44].

Eight hematology parameters in offspring at 30 days after birth were also within the normal range [42], although statistically significant differences in RBC and MCHC levels were observed between GQD groups with different doses (P < 0.05) (Fig. 4 ). Most disease states are associated with abnormal hematology values [45]. However, the hematology data for the first litters did not indicate the presence of any organ disease.

All second and third litters of offspring from various GQD-exposed groups also exhibited normal liver and kidney functions and normal hematology levels at 30 days after birth, although statistically significant differences in several parameters were observed between the groups (Fig. 4). These findings indicated the health of offspring, supported by the observation of second and third litters of mouse pups in GQD-exposed groups after birth (Figs. S5 and S6).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sex ratio of female to male offspring produced by GQD-Exposed male mice
ce:section.ce:para: We recorded the numbers of all living female and male pups in each first, second, and third litter of offspring produced by GQD-exposed and PBS-treated male mice and found that the ratios of female to male offspring in each litter were approximately 1:1 for all groups (Table S1). This finding indicates that mouse offspring generated by GQD-exposed male mice retained the natural sex ratio.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Malformations in fetuses produced by GQD-Exposed male mice
ce:section.ce:para: Malformations among mouse offspring after birth can be difficult to evaluate as some abnormal pups may be consumed by the dam. Therefore, some groups of pregnant female mice were carefully anaesthetized at approximately 2 days before parturition and fetuses were isolated. Among eight female mice mated with eight non-exposed males, 107 fetuses were produced and only one female mouse produced one malformed fetus (Fig. 5 a and b), which may have been a natural phenomenon. In the GQD-exposure groups, 90–106 fetuses were obtained from each group (eight pairs of mouse parents per group). Among the oral gavage groups, no malformed fetuses were produced by females mated with low-dose GQD-exposed males. However, in the intermediate- and high-dose groups, one and two malformed fetuses were found among the offspring, respectively (Fig. 5a and b). Among the intravenous injection groups, no malformed fetuses were found in the medium-dose GQD group; but in the low- and high-dose groups, two females (one in each group) produced two malformed fetuses (Fig. 5a and b). Each group produced 98.03 ± 0.04%–100% normal fetuses, no statistically significant differences were observed in the ratios of normal fetuses to total fetuses among the oral gavage, intravenous injection, and control groups (Fig. 5c). These results indicate that male mice exposed to different doses of GQDs via oral gavage or intravenous injection do not subsequently produce a higher proportion of malformed fetuses than non-exposed males.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The main reasons for the lack of effect of GQD exposure on male mouse reproduction and offspring health
ce:section.ce:para:
The main reasons may be as follows:
(1)
GQDs exhibited low toxicity to male germ cells

When GC-1 cells were incubated with GQDs (0.05–0.5 mg/mL) for 2–48 h, cell viabilities ranged from 96.6% to 105% (Fig. 6 a). A similar outcome was observed when TM3 cells were incubated with GQDs ≤0.3 mg/mL for 2–48 h (Fig. 6b). TM3 viability was significantly decreased compared with control (cells without treatment) only when the GQD concentration increased to 0.5 mg/mL with an incubation interval ≥24 h. These findings indicate that GQDs had low toxicity in important types of germ cells, particularly GC-1 cells.
(2)
Virtually all GQDs were excreted rapidly from the body through urine and/or feces, without migrating into the testes, epididymis, or brain following exposure.

To analyze the biodistribution of GQDs in mice, we labeled the GQDs with Cu2+ ions (Fig. 7 a), which can be firmly adsorbed by GQDs [46,47]. We calculated that 7.70 mg Cu2+ ions could be chelated by 100 mg of GQDs (see Supporting Information) and found that GQDs-Cu2+ composites were stable in simulated body fluid including SGF and SB (Fig. 7b), as determined by ICP-OES.

After mice were intravenously injected with PBS-dispersed GQDs-Cu2+ (GQDs: 10 mg/mL, 200 μL; Cu2+: 0.77 mg/mL, 200 μL), mouse testes and epididymes were found to contain markedly low concentrations of Cu2+ ions 1–24 h post-injection, but no statistically significant differences in Cu2+ concentrations in the testes and epididymes were observed between the GQDs-Cu2+-treated group and the no-injection group. In addition, no GQDs were detected in the brain based on the concentrations of Cu2+ ions (Fig. 7c). Similar results were obtained for mice orally gavaged with GQDs-Cu2+ (Fig. 7d).

Although SGF- or SB-dispersed GQDs remained transparent following storage at ambient temperature for 3 days, GQDs in SGF tended to aggregate compared with those in SB (Fig. S7). Intravenously injected materials typically directly enter the blood circulation, whereas orally administered materials first enter the stomach. Therefore, a large quantity of GQDs were detected in mouse blood, spleen, liver, lung, kidney, and bone following intravenous injection (Fig. 7c), whereas very few were detected in the blood and main organs after oral gavage (Fig. 7d). In the intravenous injection group, GQDs had been cleared from the blood by 24 h post-injection and ultimately excreted, mainly through urine, and only very small amounts of GQDs could be detected 7 days post-injection. In the oral gavage group, GQDs were excreted mainly through feces, with the majority cleared from the body by 24 h post-gavage.

The observation that GQDs-treated male mice retained normal sexual behavior and reproductivity and that female mice mated with GQDs-treated male mice produced healthy offspring may therefore be attributable to the low toxicity of GQDs in male germ cells (Fig. 7e), given that GQDs showed low uptake by the brain and the male reproductive system and were rapidly removed from body, mainly through urine and/or feces, after intravenous injection or oral gavage (Fig. 7f).
(3)
Histology of mouse testis and epididymis was not affected by GQD exposure.

Thirty days after the last oral gavage or intravenous injection with GQDs, mouse testes and epididymis were resected for analysis. As shown in Fig. 8 a, the morphologies of the testes and epididymis of mice gavaged with low, intermediate, or high doses of GQDs were similar to those of the controls. No statistically significant differences were observed in the testes and epididymis coefficients between the GQD gavage and control groups (Fig. 8b).

Most importantly, H&E staining of ultra-thin tissue sections showed no damage to the testicular and epididymal micro-structures of GQD-gavaged mice, similar to those of the controls (Fig. 8c and Fig S8). For example, the distance between the seminiferous tubules and the diameter of these tubes in the testes as well as the basement membrane and testicular interstitial tissue were similar between GQD-gavaged and control mice. Furthermore, spermatogonia, spermatocytes, spermatids, sertoli cells, and leydig cells in the tubular lumens were normal and the epididymis of high-dose GQDs-gavaged mice retained a normal epithelial layer and interstitial space while the density of mature sperm in the lumen of the epididymes was similar to that of control mice.

In male mice intravenously injected with GQDs at different doses, the morphology (Fig. 8a), organ coefficients (Fig. 8b), and histopathological structure (Fig. 8c and Fig S8) of the testis and epididymis 30 days post-injection were also similar to those of control animals.

The testes produce sperm and male sex hormones, and the epididymis stores the sperm and promotes sperm maturation [42]. Both the testes and epididymis are important components of the reproductive system in male reptiles, birds, and mammals. Therefore, normal histopathologic structure of the testes and epididymis is a fundamental requirement for long-term reproductive health. GQD exposure via oral gavage or intravenous injection had no effect on the microstructure of mouse testes and epididymis, and this observation may represent the main reason that GQD-exposed mice retained normal reproductive ability.
(4)
Normal enzyme and total protein activity in testes and epididymes.

In addition to the activities of α-glucosidase enzyme in the epididymes, the activities of several important enzymes produced by the testes, including GSH-Px, LDH, and ACP, as well as TP content, are related to sperm quality. α-glucosidase is mainly generated by the epididymes, and its activity correlates with epididymal function and sperm motility [48]. The activities of GSH-Px, LDH, and ACP and the concentration of TP in the testes also positively correlates with sperm quality [49]. For example, LDH activity significantly correlates with sperm motility, progressive motility, viability, and pathomorphology [50]. To demonstrate whether testis and epididymis functions were affected by GQD exposure, we measured the activities of these enzymes in the testes and epididymes of GQD-exposed male mice. As shown in Fig. 8d, no statistically significant differences were observed in the activities of α-glucosidase, GSH-Px, LDH, and ACP and in the concentration of TP between GQD-exposed mice and control animals. In addition, enzyme activities and TP concentration in the testes of mice treated with GQDs via oral gavage were similar to those of mice treated with GQDs via intravenous injection. For example, the activity levels of α-glucosidase in mice following oral gavage or intravenous injection with high doses of GQDs were 48.07 ± 7.24 and 66.57 ± 17.68 U/g tissue, respectively; in the control groups, these levels were 52.29 ± 7.28 (oral gavage with PBS) and 43.28 ± 20.74 U/g tissue (intravenous injection with PBS), respectively. Male mice exposed to high doses of GQDs therefore retained normal enzyme activity levels, indicating that GQDs may have little or no effect on the physiological functions of mouse testis and epididymis.
(5)
GQD-exposed mouse sperm retained high quality.

Epididymal sperm quality directly affects human and animal reproductive capacity and the health of offspring. Therefore, to further investigate why the reproductive system of male mice appeared to be undamaged by GQD exposure, epididymal sperm from GQD-exposed and PBS-treated mice were isolated and evaluated.

For the oral gavage groups, sperm morphologies in the epididymis of mice treated with GQDs at low, intermediate, and high dose levels were similar to those of control mice at 30 days after gavage (Fig. 9 a). Sperm activity can be divided into four grades: (a) rapid; (b) medium; (c) slow; and (d) static. More than half of sperm (68.67 ± 5.13%) from mice gavaged with high doses of GQDs were of grade (a) (Fig. 9b). The motility, progression, VAP, VSL, VCL, ALH, BCF, STR, and LIN of sperm from GQD-gavaged mice were similar to those of control mice. Furthermore, these results were similar to those in the intravenous injection groups (Fig. 9a and b).

Mouse sperm quality was therefore not affected by exposure to high-dose GQDs via oral gavage or intravenous injection, which may represent a further reason that GQD-exposed mice retained normal reproductive ability. Furthermore, the high quality of sperm among GQD-exposed mice not only demonstrated that the histopathological structures of the testes and epididymes were unchanged by high-dose GQD exposure but also implied that these structures retained their normal physiological functions of producing and storing healthy sperm.
(6)
Male testosterone levels in serum from GQD-exposed mice remained normal.

Sex hormones are produced by the reproductive system and regulated through the hypothalamus–pituitary–gonadal axis [51–53]. Sex hormones play important roles in motivating the sexual behavior of animals. As shown in Fig. 9c, in male mice orally gavaged or intravenously injected with GQDs at low, intermediate, or high doses, average testosterone levels ranged from 0.20 ± 0.03 to 0.28 ± 0.04 ng/mL, and were similar to those of controls (∼0.25 ng/mL).
(7)
Mice retained normal liver and kidney function and complete blood counts.

No male mice (sires) died after exposure to GQDs. We observed no obvious changes in behavior (e.g., general locomotor activity) among GQD-exposed male mice compared with control mice. Male mice exposed to low, intermediate, or high doses of GQDs via oral gavage or intravenous injection all exhibited normal dietary intake, with no obvious reduction in body weight compared with control mice in the 30 days after the last oral gavage or injection dose (Fig. 10 a).

Thirty days after the last oral gavage or intravenous injection with GQDs, blood was collected for analysis from the retro-orbital sinus of male mice and the main organs were resected after sacrifice. No changes were observed in the morphologies and histological structures of the heart, liver, spleen, lung, and kidney of high-dose GQD-exposed male mice compared with controls (Fig. 10b, c and Fig S9). In addition, no statistically significant differences were observed in the coefficients of these main organs between GQD-exposed and control groups (Fig. 10d).

As shown in Fig. 10e, serum biochemistry parameters of liver function (ALB, GLOB, A/G, AST, ALT, ALP, and TP) and of kidney function (CRE and urea) in the low-, intermediate-, and high-dose GQD-exposed groups were not statistically significantly different to those in the control group. Hematology parameters (WBC, RBC, HCT, MCV, MCH, MCHC, HGB, and PLT) were also found to be unaffected by GQD exposure compared with the control group. All serum biochemistry and hematology parameter levels in both GQD-gavaged and control groups were within normal ranges [44].

Similar results were found in the intravenous injection groups. Even where male mice had been injected with high doses of GQDs, liver and kidney function and complete blood count parameters were within normal ranges. These results indicate that male mice were in good health following oral gavage or intravenous injection with GQDs, even at high doses.

These results demonstrate, at molecular, cellular, and whole-body level, that high doses of GQDs administered by oral gavage or intravenous injection do not perturb the physiological function of the male reproductive system in mice.

The GQDs described in these experiments were prepared using a bottom-up approach. We also investigated the reproductive toxicity and in vivo biodistribution of GQDs prepared using a top-down approach, and found that GQDs prepared by cutting down graphene oxide (a top-down method; Fig. S10) also showed low toxicity in male germ cells (Fig. S11). Furthermore, these GQDs are negatively charged (−30.4 ± 2.4 eV) nanoparticles, can be labeled with Cu2+ ions (Fig. S12), do not easily penetrate the blood–brain and blood–testes barriers, and are rapidly excreted from the body after exposure (Fig. S13).

Therefore, these GQDs may also exert low toxicity to male mouse reproductive and offspring health. Other factors such as surface charge and functional groups might exert adverse effects on male mouse reproductive, however, and should be further investigated in future studies.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conclusion
ce:section.ce:para: We studied the effect of GQD exposure (intravenous injection and oral gavage) on the short- and long-term reproductive ability of male mice and on the health of their offspring. We found that GQD-exposed male mice retained normal sexual behaviors when housed with non-exposed female mice. The activities of four key enzymes expressed by the testes and epididymes were not affected by GQD exposure, and parameters of sperm quality and male sex hormones among all GQD-exposed male mice were similar to those of control mice. We also studied the development of offspring produced by GQD-exposed male mice. All female mice mated with GQDs-treated male mice had normal pregnancies and production of litters compared with controls (females mated with PBS-treated male mice). The average body weight of pups, number of pups, and sex ratio in the first litters of offspring produced by GQD-exposed groups were similar to those of offspring produced by control groups. No statistically significant differences were observed in pup growth rate, physiological structures of main organs, liver and kidney functions, or hematology parameters between the first litters of offspring in GQD-exposed and control groups. Furthermore, female mice mated with GQD-exposed males produced second and third litters of pups when continuously housed with the males. The growth rate and blood analysis data for these offspring were similar to those of the offspring produced by control groups. In addition, rates of pup deformities were similar between GQD-exposed and control groups. These findings may be attributable to the observation that GQDs prepared either by pyrolysis of l-glutamic acid powder (bottom-up approach) or by cutting graphene oxide (top-down approach) showed low toxicity in male germ cells, could not easily penetrate the blood–brain and blood–testis barriers, did not damage the histological structures and functions of testes and epididymes, and were rapidly excreted from the body after exposure, mainly through urine and/or feces. Furthermore, male mice exposed to GQDs remained in good health (e.g., liver and kidney function, hematology parameters, and histological structures of the main organs were normal). Our results indicate that high doses of GQDs administered via oral gavage or intravenous injection produce no discernible short- and long-term toxic effects on male reproductive ability and health of offspring. Given the significant potential of GQDs for in vivo drug delivery and cancer phototherapy, our results may greatly benefit future in vivo medical applications of GQDs.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported in part by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (31570960).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:section-title: Supplementary data
ce:section.ce:para: The following is the Supplementary data to this article:

This section includes detailed information on chemicals, kits, and animals; GQD synthesis and characterization; a schematic illustration of the primary experimental design; sex ratios of female to male mouse pups; all mouse parents and the growth of the first, second, and third litters of offspring during the 21 days after birth; H&E-stained slices of testes and epididymes and other main organs from male mice orally gavaged or intravenously injected with various doses of GQDs or PBS alone; stability of GQDs in simulated body fluids; and data from GQDs prepared by cutting down graphene oxide, including transmission electron microscope (TEM) images, toxicity in mouse germ cells, and time-dependent biodistribution of GQDs-Cu2+ in male mice 6–24 h post-exposure and controls.
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:section-title: Supplementary data
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.biomaterials.2018.12.001.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Recent advances in graphene quantum dots for sensing
Focusing on luminescent graphene quantum dots: current status and future perspectives
Glowing graphene quantum dots and carbon dots: properties, syntheses, and biological applications
Hierarchically targeted and penetrated delivery of drugs to tumors by size-changeable graphene quantum dot nanoaircrafts for photolytic therapy
Facile synthesis of Graphene quantum dots from 3D graphene and their application for Fe3+ sensing
